coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence based dopamine detection

Bren, Urban
Lobnik, Aleksandra
coredata.dc:description: Dopamine is an important hormone and neurotransmitter, and its levels in human fluids can indicate stress, depression, and various mental disorders. Food products, as well as medications, affect its level in the human body greatly. Therefore, dopamine monitoring is crucial, and necessary for improving the quality of life. The priority is to search for simple and environmentally friendly sensor systems for the in vitro detection of dopamine, enabling mass utilization. In this study, we explored the use of o-phthalaldehyde (OPA) as an indicator for the detection of dopamine, with fluorescence in the visible range (λex/λem = 390/455 nm), while direct dopamine fluorescence measurement was in the UV range (λex/λem = 280/320 nm). The longer excitation/emission wavelengths of dopamine-OPA complex, as well as lower detection limits, are useful for developing a simple detection method using LEDs. Three types of poloxamers were tested as additives to improve the fluorescence signal from the reaction between dopamine and OPA. Pluronic F127 led to a 16-fold increase in the fluorescence. Utilizing 4% Pluronic F127 with OPA at pH 7 resulted in a linear response within concentration ranges of dopamine (0.5–3 µM), achieving a limit of detection of 0.015 µM. In contrast, a direct detection of dopamine within the same range exhibited a detection limit of 0.13 µM.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dopamine is an important hormone and neurotransmitter, and its levels in human fluids can indicate stress, depression, and various mental disorders. Food products, as well as medications, affect its level in the human body greatly. Therefore, dopamine monitoring is crucial, and necessary for improving the quality of life. The priority is to search for simple and environmentally friendly sensor systems for the in vitro detection of dopamine, enabling mass utilization.

In this study, we explored the use of o-phthalaldehyde (OPA) as an indicator for the detection of dopamine, with fluorescence in the visible range (λex/λem = 390/455 nm), while direct dopamine fluorescence measurement was in the UV range (λex/λem = 280/320 nm). The longer excitation/emission wavelengths of dopamine-OPA complex, as well as lower detection limits, are useful for developing a simple detection method using LEDs. Three types of poloxamers were tested as additives to improve the fluorescence signal from the reaction between dopamine and OPA. Pluronic F127 led to a 16-fold increase in the fluorescence. Utilizing 4% Pluronic F127 with OPA at pH 7 resulted in a linear response within concentration ranges of dopamine (0.5–3 µM), achieving a limit of detection of 0.015 µM. In contrast, a direct detection of dopamine within the same range exhibited a detection limit of 0.13 µM.

Photographs of the dopamine-OPA complex with and without the addition of Pluronic F127 under UV light (365 nm) and Fluorescence spectra (λex=390 nm) of dopamine-OPA complex with various Pluronic F127 concentration at pH 7.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine plays a pivotal role as a neurotransmitter within the central nervous system, involved primarily in mood regulation, motivation, and the processing of stressful stimuli. Changes in dopamine levels are linked closely to various psycho-emotional states, including anxiety, depression, and stress. Neurotransmitters like dopamine, norepinephrine, serotonin, and epinephrine serve as clinically significant biomarkers for various pathological conditions [1]. Monitoring their concentrations in the human body can aid in diagnosing these conditions earlier and more accurately, as well as in assessing disease progression. In a recent study [2], significant indicators of stress were identified by monitoring the concentrations of six biomarkers in urine. The most differences were observed for dopamine and serotonin, whose concentrations were lower in individuals experiencing high levels of stress [2] or in a state of Major Depressive Disorder [3]. Study [4] demonstrated a correlation between the ratio of serotonin to dopamine levels and behavior, particularly high impulsivity. The levels of biomarker concentrations in urine, and their ratios, are being studied actively, highlighting the need for the development of multi-play sensor systems. The development of sensitive and specific methods for stress biomarker determination holds substantial importance for monitoring the effectiveness of stress-regulating therapeutic interventions. This facilitates better assessment of treatment efficacy, safety, and patient evaluation in clinical studies [5].

In medicine, a dopamine solution is used to enhance cardiac pumping strength and increase blood flow to the kidneys in shock conditions. The use of dopamine as a medication results in its presence in water bodies, including domestic and hospital effluents. Due to its potential adverse effects on aquatic fauna, a study [6] has proposed a method for purifying water from dopamine contamination.

Dopamine is naturally present in foods, with bananas being a notable source. Studies have shown that banana peels contain a high concentration of dopamine, ranging from 80 to 560 mg per 100 g, while the pulp contains approximately 2.5 to 10 mg per 100 g [7].

Measuring dopamine is promising in food products [7], pharmaceuticals [8], environmental [6], and biological objects [3]. High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) is used commonly for dopamine determination, due to its high sensitivity and specificity, making it a preferred method in numerous studies [9,10]. However, the accessibility of HPLC is limited by its requirement for expensive equipment and specialized skills, which can pose challenges for many laboratories. Additionally, HPLC methods rely on non-eco-friendly organic solvents.

Another effective method for determining dopamine in biological samples like urine and serum with high accuracy and sensitivity is the enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [11]. This technique is designed to account for a wide range of potential interferences, and is recommended for dopamine analysis in human urine. It's important to note that all dopamine concentration measurements are typically recalculated relative to urine creatinine levels [11], which necessitates additional methods for measuring creatinine. Performing ELISA requires specialized equipment and requires trained personnel.

Alternative methods for dopamine detection include electrochemical [12,13] and optical approaches [14-28]. However, electrochemical methods face numerous challenges that limit their mass utilization, including difficulties in electrode manufacture, electrode stability, and interference from substances like ascorbic and uric acids [29]. These interferences have similar oxidation potentials to dopamine, making accurate detection challenging. On the other hand, spectrophotometric methods for dopamine detection [30-32] often have relatively high limits of detection (LOD), as shown in Table 1 , rendering them unsuitable for measuring dopamine in real urine or blood samples due to their reduced sensitivity and accuracy.

The concentration of dopamine in urine ranges from 0.3 μM to 3 μM, and in blood plasma from 0 nM to 0.25 nM [1]. The authors [32,33] used the standard addition method to determine dopamine concentrations by introducing artificially spiked dopamine into diluted solutions of real serum and urine samples (spiked serum and urine) to assess the recoveries of the proposed methods. Initially, present dopamine in real samples obtained from volunteers was not detected [14-21,26-28,32,33]. This test demonstrates primarily that diluted serum and urine samples do not interfere with dopamine detection, rather than showcasing the ability to detect it. Notably, spectrophotometric methods [30-33] have shown success in measuring dopamine concentration in pharmaceutical preparations. Additionally, the method using KFeIII[FeII(CN)6] has been tested successfully for measuring dopamine concentration in bananas [33].

Assessing the selectivity of the proposed methods for dopamine detection can be challenging, particularly due to the presence of structurally similar interfering compounds, such as norepinephrine, epinephrine, l-dopa, and, partially, serotonin. These compounds share similarities with dopamine, making it difficult to differentiate them accurately. Tables 1, 2 , and 3 illustrate the range of interfering compounds tested in the proposed dopamine detection methods.

Modern developments in determining dopamine concentrations involve sensors based on enzymes, antibodies, aptamers, molecularly imprinted polymers, and nanomaterials, as described in recent reviews [29,34,35]. One promising approach is the combination of metal nanoparticles with biological components. For instance, a simple, robust, and user-friendly lateral flow test strip was developed successfully for dopamine detection in urine using aptamer-based sensing conjugated to gold nanoparticles [36]. Another highly sensitive method with the lowest limit of detection (LOD = 0.16pM) for dopamine detection was achieved by [14], utilizing the fluorescence response of copper nanoclusters with bovine serum albumin in the presence of H2O2. Various fluorogenic composite materials [14-22] for dopamine detection are summarized in Table 2.

However, the production of materials like metallic particles or quantum dots often involves labor-intensive, uneconomical, and potentially toxic processes [37]. Furthermore, modifications through the addition of components such as aptamers or other biological molecules may render these sensors less accessible and convenient for widespread use.

Despite the expansion of various sensor technologies, commercial dopamine sensors are not yet available on the market [35]. The solution requires the development of stable and standardized technologies at low costs, enabling large-scale production, extended expiration times, and, ideally, creating recyclable or multi-use testers [35].

Fluorescent response in dopamine detection methods typically exhibits several orders of magnitude higher sensitivity compared to absorption methods (as observed in Table 1 and Table 2). Furthermore, the development of devices based on light-emitting diodes (LEDs) for fixed excitation and emission wavelengths poses no significant difficulties. Therefore, the search for new, simple, and environmentally friendly fluorescent test methods for dopamine detection is highly relevant. Such methods can be used on-site without requiring additional operations or specialized skills, making them applicable across various fields, including medicine, the food industry, ecology, and beyond.

Dopamine exhibits fluorescence in the UV range [38]. Reference [39] proposed a direct method for measuring dopamine fluorescence in a methanol solution with a limit of detection of 0.082 µg/mL. However, applying this method to real human urine samples requires a specialized and complex pre-treatment procedure. In addition to efforts focused on dopamine determination, separate studies have explored various sample preparation techniques for analyzing dopamine, catecholamines, and their metabolites using different extraction methods, as described in a review [40]. Our publication introduced a novel approach for determining dopamine in aqueous solutions without the use of methanol, aiming to eliminate the use of hazardous toxic solvents in dopamine analysis.

Fluorescent methods for dopamine detection that do not require the synthesis of fluorogenic composite materials and proceeds in solution are presented in Table 3. The polymerization reaction leading to polydopamine nanoparticles was utilized for the spectrofluorimetric detection of dopamine [23,24]. This approach is environmentally friendly and fast, but has multiple steps, including the control of polymerization.

The use of resorcinol for dopamine detection offers a promising approach with an LOD of 1.8 nM [28], demonstrating high selectivity and successful testing with urine samples. In [28], using an artificial addition of dopamine allowed achieving good recovery results (97.84% - 103.50%), and the initial dopamine content in urine samples was recorded, but there was no confirmation by other methods that the result obtained was correct. The resorcinol is a promising specific indicator for dopamine, but it is very sensitive for pH, and the method proposed in [28] involves multiple steps, including pH correction.

Within our sphere of interest, the search for a solution to achieve fluorescence in a single step without additional operations is paramount. Additionally, the sensor system should possess storage stability and not contain toxic components. O-phthalaldehyde (OPA) emerges as a promising reagent, due to its solubility and stability in aqueous buffers [41], coupled with its ability to react selectively with the amino groups present in various biological amines, including dopamine. The reaction of dopamine with OPA has already been published for visualizing this neurotransmitter in cells, and has been utilized in microscopic observations [42]. However, the complexes resulting from the dopamine-OPA reaction exhibit relatively low fluorescence intensity, as will be demonstrated in this publication. Variations, including additional components in the reaction environment, have unlocked new possibilities for utilizing OPA as a dopamine indicator. Notably, there have been no studies on utilizing OPA for the quantitative detection of dopamine using fluorescence methods, highlighting a novel area of research with significant potential.
The fluorescence-based method presented in this publication applies a simple solution measurement system based on the reaction between dopamine and OPA in the presence of Pluronic F127 at pH 7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
Chemicals
ce:section.ce:para: Phthaldialdehyde – o-Phthalaldehyde (≥99.0% (HPLC), Dopamine hydrochloride (Pharmaceutical Secondary Standard), Melatonin (Pharmaceutical Secondary Standard), Serotonin hydrochloride (≥98%), l-Norepinephrine hydrochloride (≥98.0%), l-Kynurenine (≥98%, for HPLC), Octopamine hydrochloride, l-Alanine, Arginine hydrochloride, l-Cysteine hydrochloride monohydrate, Glycine, l-Glutamine, Urea, l-Ascorbic acid, l-Glucose, Pluronic L31, Pluronic 10R5, Pluronic F127, potassium hydrogen phthalate (99.5%), NaH2PO4 (≥98%) and NaHCO3 (99.7%) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich.
Ethanol absolute (99.98%), NaOH (99%), KH2PO4 (99%) were obtained from Gram Mol. HCl (37% Honeywell Fluka) and Na2HPO4 (Merck ACS, Reag.PhEur).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Buffer solutions
ce:section.ce:para: The buffer solutions were prepared in the next ratios: pH 3: 100 mL 0.1 M potassium hydrogen phthalate and 44.6 mL of 0.1 M HCl; pH 5: 100 mL 0.1 M potassium hydrogen phthalate and 45.2 mL of 0.1 M NaOH; pH 6: 100 mL 0.1 M KH2PO4 and 11.2 mL of 0.1 M NaOH; pH 7: 100 mL 0.1 M KH2PO4 and 58.2 mL of 0.1 M NaOH; pH 8: 100 mL 0.1 M KH2PO4 and 93.4 mL of 0.1 M NaOH; pH 9.6: 100 mL 0.05 M NaHCO3 and 10 mL of 0.1 M NaOH; pH 11: 100 mL 0.05 M Na2HPO4 and 8.2 mL of 0.1 M NaOH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Work solution composition
ce:section.ce:para: Direct measuring of dopamine (5 µM): 2 mL of Buffer solution; 5.5 mL of distilled water and 2.5 mL of dopamine water solution (conc. 2 × 10−5 M).
Dopamine (50 µM) and OPA (500 µM): 2 mL of Buffer solution; 0.25 mL of OPA solution in EtOH (conc. 2 × 10−2 M); 5.25 mL of distilled water and 2.5 mL of dopamine water solution (conc. 2 × 10−4 M).

Dopamine (50 µM) and OPA (500 µM) and 2% Pluronic: 2 mL of Buffer solution; 0.25 mL of OPA solution in EtOH (conc. 2 × 10−2 M); 2 mL of Pluronic water solution (10% conc.); 3.25 mL of distilled water and 2.5 mL of dopamine water solution (conc. 2 × 10−4 M).

Dopamine (5 µM) and OPA (500 µM) and 4% Pluronic F127: 2 mL of Buffer solution; 0.25 mL of OPA solution in EtOH (conc. 2 × 10−2 M); 4 mL of Pluronic F127 water solution (10% conc.); 1.25 mL of distilled water and 2.5 mL of dopamine water solution (conc. 2 × 10−5 M).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Instrumentation
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra were measured on a Fluorescence Spectrometer LS55 (Perkin Elmer). The measurement parameters on the spectrometer, such as the power of the lamp, excitation and emission slit, were adjusted to the intensity of the observed fluorescence. The direct measurements of dopamine were recorded at the high lamp power, the excitation and emission slits were 10 nm (will be indicated as H-10/10). The product measurements of the reaction of dopamine with OPA were recorded at medium lamp power, and the excitation and emission slits were 3 nm or 5 nm (will be indicated as M-3/3 or M-5/5). The fluorescence spectra of dopamine (50 µM) with OPA (500 µM) under pH 5 were recorded at high lamp power, and the excitation and emission slits were 10 nm (H-10/10). The fluorescence spectra were recorded at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculations
ce:section.ce:para: The Standard Deviation (SD) was calculated using the formula SD=√{Σ(IF−MV)2/n}, where IF is the intensity of the fluorescence, and MV is the mean value of n measurements of the fluorescence intensity.
The Relative Standard Deviation (RSD) was calculated using the formula RSD=100% × SD/MV.

The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantitation (LOQ) were determined based on the Standard Deviation of the blank response and the slope from the linear equation of the calibration curve: LOD=3 × SD/k, LOQ=10 × SD/k, where SD is the Standard Deviation of 5 blank samples, k is the slope of the linear equation IF = m + k × [DA], and [DA] – is the concentration of dopamine, mol/L.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Study of direct fluorescence dopamine detection
Effect of pH on dopamine fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: Various pH values were probed (pH 3-pH 11) to determine the optimal pH for dopamine fluorescence spectra. Comparative spectra were also recorded for blank solutions containing the respective buffers without dopamine. The dopamine concentration was set at 5 µM. The fluorescence spectra at various pH values are presented in Fig. 1 .
At pH 3 and pH 5 no fluorescence was observed; the spectrum of the dopamine solution coincided with the blank signal and was at the noise level (the light blue and light green lines, Fig. 1).
At pH 11 there was also no fluorescence, only the Raman scattering signal was detectable at 310 nm upon excitation at 280 nm (the purple line, Fig. 1)

The dopamine fluorescence was detected at pH 7 λex/λem=280/320 nm (the solid magenta line, Fig. 1). The blank solution at pH 7 exhibited a Raman scattering signal in the spectrum (the dotted magenta line, Fig. 1).

According to [39], dopamine exhibits fluorescence in the pH range of 2 to 8, while the oxidation of dopamine occurs at pH values above 10, resulting in the formation of a non-fluorescent product. We observed a similar trend in our study, except that fluorescence was absent in the acidic environment. It seems that not only pH, but also the buffer composition, affect the fluorescent properties. In reference [39], an acetate buffer was used to create the acidic environment, while, in our experiment, the phthalate buffer was employed. Therefore, phthalate ions in the acidic environment seem to hinder the fluorescence emission of dopamine.

Since dopamine fluorescence at pH 7 exhibits a high enough intensity to identify the presence of dopamine, we applied a buffer at pH 7 to study the dependence of fluorescence intensity on the dopamine concentration further.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of dopamine fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: Spectra with various dopamine concentrations (0.5–5 µM) at pH 7 are shown in Fig. 2 A. The graph (Fig. 2B) depicts that the fluorescence output was directly proportional to the dopamine concentration. Based on the data obtained from the calibration curve, the limit of detection was calculated at 0.13 µM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference study for the direct dopamine fluorescence method
ce:section.ce:para: UV wavelengths λex/λem = 280/320 nm lack specificity, as various organic compounds exhibit fluorescence in this range. Fig. 3 provides a comparison of dopamine's fluorescence with other hormones, such as serotonin, norepinephrine, kynurenine, and melatonin.

The fluorescence intensity of dopamine (λex/λem = 280/320 nm) is similar to the one of norepinephrine. Kynurenine, excited at λex = 280 nm, does not exhibit any fluorescence. In contrast, the fluorescence of serotonin and melatonin excited at λex = 280 nm surpassed that of dopamine significantly, and extended well beyond the measurement range of the instrument.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent properties of the reaction product between dopamine and opa
ce:section.ce:para: A direct fluorescence-based determination of dopamine in real human fluid samples, considering that other hormones influence the signal intensity significantly, is not feasible without a special sample pre-treatment. It is evident that even low concentrations of melatonin or, although to a lesser extent, serotonin, can overshadow the dopamine signal completely. In the study [39], a direct fluorescence method for dopamine analysis was applied for urine samples; however, the sample pre-processing involved a complex and labor-intensive scheme. Employing suitable sample preparation techniques before instrument detection to enrich the target analytes and to remove the interferences is a practicable and widespread approach [40].

An indicator capable of forming a fluorophore with lower excitation and emission energy in the visible range than the intrinsic dopamine fluorescence can be used for developing a fluorescent detection method of dopamine. This can facilitate the development of portable detection systems with simple LEDs.

The mechanism of the nucleophilic substitution reaction of OPA remains not fully understood [43]. In [42], a mass spectrometric analysis of the reaction products of dopamine and OPA was published, along with the proposed structural formulas. The presumed products are depicted in Fig. 4 . Additionally, higher molecular masses were also detected in the mass spectra [42], suggesting that the reaction proceeds further, giving rise to dimers and trimers. It is possible that the further reaction process leads to a change in the fluorescent properties of the formed products, providing some dynamics of the fluorescent signal specific for dopamine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent response of dopamine-opa reaction to pH variation
ce:section.ce:para: Acidity exhibits an effect on the reaction between dopamine and OPA. At pH 5, very low fluorescence was observed, with excitation and emission wavelengths at 390 nm and 460 nm respectively (Fig. 5 ). The excitation spectra exhibited its maximum intensity at 390 nm and a shoulder at 330 nm. The fluorescence intensity increased over time (+25% over 80 min).
Fig. 6 depicts the dependence of fluorescence intensity on the reaction product formed between OPA and dopamine across the pH range of 5 to 9.6 over time.

The highest fluorescence intensity was observed at pH 7 five minutes after the start of the reaction, but it was not stable. At pH 6, the signal was relatively stable, but its fluorescence intensity was only half of that at pH 7. At pH 8, the fluorescence intensity experienced a significant decrease within the first five minutes of the reaction. At pH 5 and 6, the emission maximum was at 455 nm; at pH 7 and 8, it shifted to 530 nm, and at pH 9.6, the fluorescence was absent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of additives on the fluorescent response of a dopamine-OPA reaction
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence intensity of the dopamine-OPA complex (at pH 7) is approximately 4 times weaker than the direct fluorescence of dopamine. Moreover, it is not stable over time. In this study, we examined the potential influence of additives on the fluorescence intensity of the complex. For example, the presence of 20% alcohol in the reaction medium caused a 3-fold increase in intensity (Fig. 7 ). However, the signal disappeared completely within 5 min. We hypothesized that poloxamers (nonionic triblock copolymers) could enhance the signal significantly. These molecules consist of hydrophilic ethylene oxide units (PEO) and hydrophobic propylene oxide units (PPO). They can form micelles and enter into solvation, affecting the rate and the mechanism of the reaction. To test this hypothesis, we investigated the influence of the presence of 2% poloxamers in the reaction medium on the fluorescence of the dopamine-OPA complex (Fig. 7). Pluronic L31 [(PEO)2(PPO)16(PEO)2], a reverse triblock copolymer Pluronic 10R5 [(PPO)8(PEO)22(PPO)8], and Pluronic F127 [(PEO)100(PPO)65(PEO)100] with a high molecular mass were used as additives.

Pluronic L31 and 10R5 increased the fluorescence slightly, and, at the same time, stabilized it (Fig. 7); after 5 min there was no sharp decay of fluorescence, compared to the case in the absence of these poloxomers.

An eightfold increase in fluorescence intensity of the dopamine-OPA complex was observed with 2% Pluronic F127. Photographs of the solutions of the dopamine-OPA complex at pH 7 with and without the addition of Pluronic F127 are presented in Fig. 8 . A similar effect was not observed if Pluronic F127 was introduced into the reaction medium 10 min after the start of the reaction. It is necessary that the reaction between OPA and dopamine occurs in the presence of Pluronic F127. Therefore, solutions were prepared for the experiment in the following order: buffer solution, Pluronic solution, OPA solution, and, last of all, the dopamine solution. The solutions of OPA with Pluronic F127 and dopamine with Pluronic F127 did not emit fluorescence, as seen from Fig. 8, as it only reflected the light from the lamp.

It is probable that, during the dimerization and subsequent polymerization of the reaction products between dopamine and OPA, the fluorescence intensity decreases. However, the presence of poloxamers, which form a solvated micellar shell, hinders the further progress of the reaction.

Enhancing fluorescence with surfactants is not a novel concept. For instance, Triton X, a non-ionic surfactant, has been reported to increase the fluorescence of the Al-Morin complex by over threefold [44]. However, there is a lack of analogous information regarding the effects of Pluronic F127.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent properties of the reaction product between dopamine and OPA in the presence of pluronic F127
Effect of pluronic F127 concentrations on the fluorescent response of a dopamine-OPA reaction
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the strong effect of 2% Pluronic F127 on the fluorescence of the dopamine-OPA complex, further analyses were conducted, to investigate the influence of higher Pluronic F127 concentrations.

The fluorescence spectra, obtained 5 min after initiating the reaction between dopamine and OPA with varying Pluronic F127 concentrations (0%, 2%, 4%, and 6%) in the reaction medium, are presented in Fig. 9 .

A fluorescence intensity of 250 a.u. was achieved with 2%, of 580 a.u. with 4%, and 445 a.u. with 6% Pluronic F127, while the dopamine-OPA complex exhibited a fluorescence intensity of only 35 a.u. in the absence of Pluronic F127.

The most substantial enhancement, more than 16-fold, in the fluorescence intensity of the dopamine-OPA complex was observed with 4% Pluronic F127, making it the optimal concentration for further investigations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on the fluorescent response of a dopamine-opa reaction in the presence of pluronic f127
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of pH on the fluorescence response of the reaction between dopamine and OPA was investigated previously (Fig. 6). The highest fluorescence intensity was achieved at pH 7. However, the presence of an additional Pluronic F127 component could affect the pH dependence. Therefore, a study was conducted to investigate the effect of pH on the fluorescent response of the dopamine-OPA reaction in the presence of 4% Pluronic F127 in the reaction medium. Fig. 10 presents the fluorescence spectra of dopamine-OPA complexes obtained under different pH conditions (pH 6, pH 7, pH 8, and pH 9.6). So, in this range, with increasing pH, a shift in the emission wavelength was observed from 455 nm to 525 nm. This shift can likely be attributed to the formation of two different fluorophores resulting from the reaction between dopamine and OPA (see Fig. 4). Based on the shape of the spectra in Fig. 10, it can be inferred that, at pH 6, predominantly one type of fluorophore is formed, while, at pH 7 and 8, a mixture of both fluorophore types was observed, and, primarily, the second fluorophore type was formed at pH 9.6.

The highest fluorescence intensity for the dopamine-OPA complex in the presence of 4% Pluronic F127 was observed at pH 7 5 min after the start of the reaction. The spectrum had maximum intensity at 455 nm and a shoulder at 520 nm. A decrease in dopamine concentration resulted in the attenuation of the shoulder (Fig. 10). It can be hypothesized that, at dopamine concentrations below 5 μM, the reaction leads primarily to the formation of the first fluorophore with λex/λem = 390/455 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the OPA concentration on the fluorescent response of a dopamine-OPA reaction in the presence of pluronic f127
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 11 shows a comparison of the fluorescence spectra obtained for the same concentration of dopamine, but at different concentrations of OPA in the presence of 4% Pluronic F127. The spectra were recorded 5 min after the start of the reaction. The molar ratios dopamine:OPA were 1:20 and 1:100. The intensity of the spectra differed by 13%, with the 1:100 ratio exhibiting a higher intensity. According to [29], a ratio of 1:18 is sufficient for the determination of amino acids using OPA. However, the reference does not specify that a higher concentration can provide a more intense signal.

The blank solutions containing 100 µM or 500 µM OPA, in the presence of 4% Pluronic F127 at pH 7, did not exhibit fluorescence (λex/λem = 390/455 nm) and resembled the signal of distilled water closely; weak Raman scattering was observed at 450 nm. The absence of fluorescence in the blank solution in the area of interest provided a more stable background, contributing to the increased measurement accuracy and reproducibility. Additionally, it simplified the analysis as there was no need to subtract the background signal.

Both concentrations of OPA provided a good response with a practically insignificant difference and can be used. However, due to the higher intensity of the obtained complex, 500 µM was more promising for further investigations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and dopamine concentration in the system dopamine-OPA-pluronic F127
ce:section.ce:para: The optimal conditions for dopamine determination were identified based on the experiments described above. The pH value of 7 was determined to be the most favorable for the OPA and dopamine reaction, resulting in the highest fluorescence intensity compared to other pH values. A concentration of 500 µM OPA was selected for the same reason. The most significant enhancement in fluorescence was also observed with 4% Pluronic F127.

A verification of the linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and dopamine concentration was conducted under these optimized conditions. The fluorescence spectra at various dopamine concentrations, along with the resulting calibration curve, are presented in Fig. 12 . The concentration range of 0.5–3.0 µM dopamine have a linear relationship with a high adjusted R-squared (Adj.R2 is 0.995). The calculated LOD was 15 nM and LOQ – 50 nM.
These results affirm the specificity of the interaction between dopamine and OPA, validating the efficacy of the developed method for dopamine concentration determination.

The fluorescence intensity of dopamine reaction with OPA in the presence of 4% Pluronic F127 at pH 7 changed over time and reached its maximum at the 5th minute (Fig. 13 A). Time drive plots were obtained for various dopamine concentrations and a calibration curve was constructed (Fig. 13B). The equation describing the linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and dopamine concentration matches the one obtained from the spectra closely (Fig. 12B), with the slope identical and equal to 1.09 × 108. The Standard Deviation calculated from the time drive plots of 5 blank solution samples (SD=0.40) was slightly lower than the one obtained from the spectra (SD=0.54), causing decreasing of the values of the limit of detection and limit of quantitation (LOD = 11 nM; LOQ = 37 nM).

For a more in-depth analysis of the above time drive graphs, a differentiation was used, which describes the rate of the change in fluorescence intensity over time (Fig. 14 ). Extremes, where the derivative equals zero, indicate the maxima of the time drive graphs and are practically independent of dopamine concentration, occurring at 5 min (Fig. 14A). For a greater clarity, differential curves are presented divided by the corresponding dopamine concentration (Fig. 14B). In the concentration range from 1 to 5 µM, there is a coincidence of fluorescence intensity change rates, while at concentrations from 1 µM to 50 nM, there is a shift in the extremes from 5 to 10 min, which is likely due to the fact that nanomolar concentrations of dopamine require somewhat more time to form complexes. Differentiation of time drive graphs demonstrates a high determinism in the change of complex fluorescence intensity over time and its predictability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The practical lowest concentration at which the dopamine can be detected using the OPA-Pluronic F127 detecting system
ce:section.ce:para: Solutions with concentrations of 50 nM and 5 nM were prepared, to determine the lowest concentration practically detectable in the reaction of dopamine with OPA in the presence of Pluronic F127 at pH 7. To measure the fluorescence intensity at such low concentrations, measurements were conducted at the maximal setting of the spectrofluorimeter (H-10/10). The spectra were recorded 5 min after mixing all the ingredients, and then every minute, to capture the intensity fluctuations over time. The obtained fluorescence intensity values are presented in Table 4 .

In a normal distribution, 68.27% of the obtained values should fall within the range of MV ± SD; 95.45% of the obtained values should fall within the range of MV ± 2SD; and 99.73% of the obtained values should fall within the range of MV ± 3SD (the three-sigma rule “68–95–99.7″). The intensity range 166.78±6.49 of the solution containing 5 nM dopamine intersected with the range of the blank solution 156.21± 9.18 (Table 4), therefore, it was not possible to identify the concentration of 5 nM dopamine using the OPA-Pluronic F127 detecting system with 99.7% probability, while it could be detected with 95.45% probability.

The fluorescence intensity mean value for 50 nM dopamine differed from the mean value of the blank solution by 114 a.u., which was higher than the fluctuations in the blank solution (see Table 4). It can be asserted that the 50 nM concentration of dopamine can be detected with satisfactory accuracy.

It should be noted that the LOD obtained from the calibration curve using spectrofluorimeter conditions M-5/5 is not applicable for a lower range at H-10/10. The physical meaning of LOD lies in indicating the theoretically minimum concentration below which it is not possible to identify an analyte under specific conditions and parameters of the spectrofluorimeter, relying on the fluctuations of blank values and the slope of the calibration curve. Thus, with an increase in data precision, the limit concentration decreased. The obtained data collected in Table 4 demonstrate the practical feasibility of applying the OPA-Pluronic F127 detecting system for nanomolar concentrations of dopamine by adapting the instrument parameters and concentration values for calibration to the required ranges.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of the indicator mixture for dopamine detection system OPA-Pluronic F127 pH 7
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the stability of the indicator mixture and its suitability for long-term use, a study was conducted comparing the values obtained from five measurements of a solution containing 5 µM dopamine (Table 5 ). This comparison involved the indicator mixture prepared on the same day after 4 h, and the one prepared 4 months ago and stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C.

The indicator mixture consisted of 500 µM OPA and 4% Pluronic F127 in a buffer solution at pH 7. To prepare the indicator mixture, the following solutions were mixed: 20 mL of buffer solution pH 7; 2.5 mL of 0.02 M OPA solution in EtOH; 40 mL of 10% Pluronic F127 water solution. The ratio of the indicator mixture to the analyzed solution was 1:0.6 (by volume). The concentration of dopamine in the analyzed solution was 5 µM. Therefore, by mixing it with the indicator mixture, the analyzed solution was diluted, resulting in 1.875 µM dopamine in the reaction mixture, which fels within the calibration range.

The measurements were conducted as follows: 2 mL of the indicator mixture was added to a cuvette for analysis, followed by 1.2 mL of the analyzed solution, and the time was recorded. After five minutes, the fluorescence intensity was measured, and the concentration was calculated using the calibration curve equation. A separate calibration curve was plotted for each indicator mixture.

It should be noted that the fluorescence intensity of the resulting complex may vary slightly, depending on different batches of OPA. However, having individual calibration allows for neutralizing any differences in the intensity of the complex. The fluorescence intensity of the 5 µM dopamine complex with the old 4-month indicator mixture was 13% higher than that with the 4-hour indicator mixture, indicating no loss of solution efficiency over time, and suggesting a longer shelf life for the indicator mixture.

Furthermore, the calculated values of the Relative Standard Deviation (RSD) for the 4-month indicator mixture were 1.39%, which was half of that for the 4-hour indicator mixture. The RSD of the pipette used to introduce the analyzed solution was 0.45%, indicating that fluctuations of 0.45% were inevitable, due to the solution preparation process. To obtain more consistent results, we recommend using the indicator mixture the next day after preparation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference study for the OPA-Pluronic F127 detection system
ce:section.ce:para: The developed method for dopamine concentration determination was also tested for other chemicals, to evaluate the feasibility and selectivity of the proposed method. Norepinephrine, serotonin, melatonin, kynurenine, octopamine, alanine, arginine, cysteine, glycine, glutamine which contained an amino group, could react with OPA. Under the same conditions as for dopamine (OPA 500 µM, 4% Pluronic F127, pH 7, λex/λem = 390/455 nm, 5 min reaction), the fluorescence values were measured for 5 µM of thus different analytes. The obtained fluorescence intensities are depicted in Fig. 15 . Norepinephrine and serotonin interacted with OPA, exhibiting a lower fluorescence signal compared to the one of dopamine. A fluorescence response was absent for kynurenine, melatonin, octopamine, alanine, arginine, cysteine, glycine, and glutamine.

The concentration of some interfering substances, such as urea, ascorbic acid, glucose, NaCl, CaCl2, NaH2PO4 was 0.1 M. As shown in Fig. 15, the interfering substances tested did not produce a fluorescent signal, even at such a high concentration.
Consequently, the developed method for dopamine concentration determination can be also used for the analysis of norepinephrine and serotonin.

Dopamine, norepinephrine and serotonin have different kinetic properties in the reaction with OPA in the presence of Pluronic F127 at pH 7; they all exhibit unique profiles of fluorescence time drive graphs (Fig. 16 ). The intensity of fluorescence is dependent on the reaction time. For the reaction between dopamine and OPA, the fluorescence reached its maximum intensity at the 5th minute. In the case of norepinephrine, the intensity decreased much more slowly than in the case of dopamine (Fig. 16), while, for serotonin, the fluorescence intensity increased gradually up to 15 min. Hence, different hormones exhibit varying rates of fluorescence changes. Fig. 16 also presents the fluorescence time drive plots obtained for the systemы with two or three hormones: “dopamine + norepinephrine”, “dopamine + serotonin”, “norepinephrine + serotonin” and “dopamine + norepinephrine + serotonin”. The concentration of each hormone was 5 µM. The rates of fluorescence intensity change for the hormone mixtures reflect the individual tendencies of the hormones.

Based on the obtained data (Fig. 16), it can be argued that it is possible to identify patterns by which the concentrations of dopamine, serotonin and norepinephrine could be determined by following the rate of change in fluorescence intensity resulting from the reaction with the indicator mixture.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: The intrinsic fluorescence of dopamine (λex/λem = 280 nm/320 nm) was employed for its quantitative determination, with a limit of detection of 0.13 µM at pH 7. Serotonin and melatonin exhibited a significantly higher intensity of their fluorescence at the same wavelength, which interfered with the determination of dopamine in their presence.

The reaction between dopamine and OPA proved to be a promising approach for dopamine detection. OPA acts as a specific indicator, forming a fluorescence product through the reaction with dopamine. Different buffers can hinder or enhance the fluorescent emission, indicating the importance of buffer selection in the experimental design. The pH 7 was found to be the highest fluorescence response for the reaction between OPA and dopamine.

The addition of Pluronic F127 enhanced the fluorescence intensity of the dopamine-OPA complex. Remarkably, the presence of 4% Pluronic F127 led to a more than 16-fold increase in fluorescence intensity compared to the dopamine-OPA complex alone.

It was found that the fluorescence intensity of the dopamine-OPA complex (λex/λem=390/455 nm) in the presence of 4% Pluronic F127 at pH 7 was linearly dependent on the dopamine concentration. This provides a basis for quantitative analysis and detection of dopamine using fluorescence spectroscopy. Constructing a calibration curve and calculating the limit of detection facilitated accurate and sensitive determination of the dopamine concentration, as the LOD was 15 nM. The lowest practical detection concentration of dopamine was 5 nM, with 95% probability of detecting.

The indicator mixture of 500 µM OPA and 4% Pluronic F127 at pH 7 was stable during the 4 month storage at 4 °C, and gave a good fluorescence response for dopamine. It is also possible to use this indicator mixture for measuring norepinephrine and serotonin, and can be used as a part of a multiple detecting system.
